verbatim_text: Beyond that, it gets a bit more vague, but it could be things like you have a more groundbreaking scientific contribution that wins you an award or something. And then level five is that you have an agent that autonomously or maybe non-autonomously helps guide or produce its own long-term research agenda that keeps winning awards and maybe gets someone a Turing Award or something like that. And so the natural question is where we actually are currently with these research agents within machine learning. And the answer, unfortunately, is that we're roughly at level one. So we're still basically slightly improving the baselines and we haven't even moved to these hard scientific questions yet. And currently with ML Gym, we kind of found that the best agents kind of just take these baselines and tune the hyperparameters a little bit and you get an improvement, but it's not an interesting scientific contribution. And there's another challenge with benchmarking these things. You might have very incompatible metrics across a diverse array of what you expect this agent to be able to do. And so you might expect it to help you with equation formatting in LaTeX or something. And you might also, on the other hand, expect it to help designing experiments or even running experiments or actually acting as a PDE solver itself, if we're talking about flex agents. And there are several things you can do. You can formulate these tasks. They each have their own evaluation metrics. For PDEs, this might be the L2 relative error.
